front: What two elements must BOTH be present to trigger the requirement of Miranda warnings (the instructor's 'two C's')?
back: Custody AND interrogation. Custody = objective, arrest-equivalent restraint of freedom of action; interrogation = express questioning or its functional equivalent. Warnings are not required unless both coincide.

front: Statements obtained during custodial interrogation without warnings or a valid waiver are inadmissible in the {{c1::prosecution's case-in-chief}}.
back: the prosecution's case-in-chief — the Miranda exclusionary remedy bars unwarned custodial-interrogation statements from the government's case-in-chief.

front: What did Miranda v. Arizona hold regarding statements from custodial interrogation?
back: Such statements are inadmissible unless police first gave the warnings and the suspect knowingly and voluntarily waived them.

front: How did Miranda v. Arizona originally describe 'custody'?
back: A suspect 'taken into custody or otherwise deprived of his freedom of action in any significant way.'

front: What is the benchmark for Miranda 'custody' under the objective test?
back: Whether a reasonable person's freedom of action is restrained to the degree associated with formal arrest, judged by the objective circumstances — not the officer's private view.

front: How did Rhode Island v. Innis define 'interrogation' under Miranda?
back: Interrogation is not limited to express questioning; it also includes the 'functional equivalent' — any words or actions by police (other than those normally attendant to arrest and custody) that police should know are reasonably likely to elicit an incriminating response from the suspect.

front: Innis: the functional-equivalent test reaches words or actions by police that they {{c1::should know are reasonably likely to elicit an incriminating response}} from the suspect.
back: should know are reasonably likely to elicit an incriminating response — Innis, 446 U.S. at 300-01.

front: Under Innis, whose perceptions does the 'functional equivalent of interrogation' test focus on?
back: The perceptions of the suspect — the question is whether police should know their words or actions are reasonably likely to elicit an incriminating response, not their actual intent.

front: Which case held that officers' in-car conversation about a missing shotgun and nearby handicapped children was NOT the functional equivalent of interrogation?
back: Rhode Island v. Innis — the officers should not have known the exchange was reasonably likely to elicit an incriminating response, so it was not interrogation.

front: What did Dickerson v. United States hold about Miranda and 18 U.S.C. § 3501?
back: Miranda is a constitutional rule that Congress cannot override by statute, so § 3501 cannot supersede it; Miranda governs the admissibility of confessions in federal court. This remains current law.

front: Why does Dickerson v. United States matter to the status of Miranda?
back: It settled that Miranda is a constitutional decision (not a mere judicially created prophylactic rule), so Congress's attempt to replace it with a voluntariness statute (§ 3501) was unconstitutional.

front: What two distinct things did Berkemer v. McCarty hold?
back: (1) Miranda applies to ALL custodial interrogation regardless of offense severity, misdemeanors included; and (2) the temporary, public detention of an ordinary traffic stop is NOT Miranda custody.

front: Is an ordinary traffic stop Miranda custody?
back: No. Under Berkemer v. McCarty a temporary, public roadside detention is not custody, so on-scene questioning needs no warnings — even though Miranda otherwise reaches all offenses.

front: Does the severity of the offense (misdemeanor vs. felony) affect whether Miranda applies?
back: No. Berkemer v. McCarty holds Miranda reaches all custodial interrogation regardless of whether the charge is a misdemeanor or a felony.

front: In Oregon v. Mathiason, was a suspect who voluntarily came to the station and was told he was free to leave 'in custody' for Miranda?
back: No. A voluntary station-house interview where the suspect is told he is not under arrest and free to leave is not custody — even inside a police building and even when confronted with a false claim of evidence.

front: What did Orozco v. Texas establish about the location of Miranda custody?
back: Custody is not limited to the stationhouse — four officers questioning an arrested suspect in his own bedroom in the early morning was custody requiring warnings.

front: In Orozco v. Texas, what fact made the bedroom questioning custodial?
back: By the officer's own testimony the suspect 'was under arrest and not free to leave when he was questioned in his bedroom in the early hours of the morning.'

front: Contrast Mathiason and Orozco: why was the station visit non-custodial but the bedroom questioning custodial?
back: Setting is not dispositive — restraint is. In Mathiason the suspect came voluntarily and was told he was free to leave (no custody); in Orozco he was under arrest, surrounded by four officers, and not free to leave (custody), even though it was his own home.

front: What did J.D.B. v. North Carolina add to the Miranda custody analysis?
back: A child's age is a relevant factor in the objective custody analysis when it was known to or objectively apparent to the officer, because a reasonable child may feel restrained where an adult would not.

front: Did J.D.B. v. North Carolina turn the custody test into a subjective inquiry?
back: No. Custody stays objective; a child's age is folded into that objective calculus only when known or apparent to the officer — it does not become a subjective test about the particular suspect's actual state of mind.

front: Does imprisonment by itself put an inmate 'in custody' for Miranda when questioned about an outside offense?
back: No. Howes v. Fields holds imprisonment alone is not Miranda custody; whether an inmate is in custody depends on the totality — here, told he could leave and return to his cell, he was not in custody.

front: What did Stansbury v. California hold about how custody is determined?
back: Custody is determined by the objective circumstances of the interrogation, not by the subjective, undisclosed views of the officer or suspect; an officer's uncommunicated suspicion is irrelevant.

front: Which case holds that an officer's hidden belief that a person is a suspect is irrelevant to whether that person is in Miranda custody?
back: Stansbury v. California — custody turns on objective circumstances, not the officer's uncommunicated suspicion.

front: What is the public-safety exception recognized in New York v. Quarles?
back: When officers ask questions reasonably prompted by an immediate threat to public safety (e.g., the location of a hidden loaded gun), the unwarned answers and the resulting evidence (the gun) are admissible.

front: How narrow is the Quarles public-safety exception?
back: Narrow — it is limited to questions reasonably prompted by an immediate danger; it is not a license for routine investigative questioning or general fact-gathering.

front: Why did Illinois v. Perkins hold that no Miranda warnings are required for undercover or jailhouse-informant questioning?
back: Because there is no police-dominated 'coercive atmosphere' — a suspect who does not know he is speaking to law enforcement faces no compelling pressure, so the Miranda predicate is absent.

front: Which case removes undercover agents posing as fellow inmates from Miranda's reach entirely?
back: Illinois v. Perkins — incarceration plus questioning by an undisclosed government agent does not create the coercive atmosphere Miranda guards against.

front: Do volunteered, spontaneous statements require Miranda warnings?
back: No. Volunteered statements are not the product of interrogation, so they need no warnings — even after arrest, words 'normally attendant to arrest and custody' do not count as interrogation (Innis).

front: Common pitfall: officers treat Miranda as covering all police contact. When do warnings actually attach?
back: Only when custody AND interrogation coincide. On-scene, consensual, or otherwise non-custodial questioning requires no warnings.

front: Distinction pitfall: is a Terry stop or traffic stop a Fourth Amendment seizure the same as Miranda custody?
back: No. A brief, public investigative detention is a Fourth Amendment seizure of the person, but it is NOT Miranda custody (Berkemer). Miranda custody requires arrest-equivalent restraint.

front: Common pitfall: reading the Quarles public-safety exception too broadly. What is the limit?
back: It is confined to questions neutralizing an immediate danger; it does not authorize general investigative fact-gathering.

front: What is the analytical sequence for deciding whether Miranda warnings were required for a statement?
back: (1) Was the suspect in custody (objective, arrest-equivalent restraint)? If no, no warnings. (2) Was there interrogation (express or functional equivalent under Innis)? If no, no warnings. (3) Did a public-safety danger justify unwarned questions (Quarles)? If yes, no warnings; otherwise warnings were required.

front: Contrast Perkins and Orozco: both involve a suspect already in police hands, so why does Orozco require warnings but Perkins does not?
back: Miranda guards against police-dominated coercion. In Orozco the suspect knew he was under arrest and facing officers (coercive custody → warnings). In Perkins the suspect did not know he was talking to law enforcement, so there was no coercive atmosphere and Miranda did not apply.
